messages.content:
Переведи на формальный английский так, чтобы максимально лаконично звучало для носителей языка. Где нужно измени и подправь. Чтобы было правильно для носителей "Недавно Король Дании Фредерик и королева Дании Мария опубликовали свои первые официальные портреты в качестве монархов после того, как Queen Margrethe II отреклась от престола в пользу своего старшего сына. 

14 января Фредерик был провозглашен новым королем теперь, после 3 месяцев пребывания у власти, королевская чета представила свои инаугурационные портреты."

Recently, King Frederik and Queen Mary of Denmark released their inaugural official portraits as monarchs following Queen Margrethe II’s abdication in favor of her eldest son. On January 14, Frederik was proclaimed the new king; now, after three months in power, the royal couple have unveiled their inauguration portraits.
как правильно читается "HM Queen Margrethe"?
"HM Queen Margrethe" читается как "Her Majesty Queen Margrethe".

Переведи на формальный английский так, чтобы максимально лаконично звучало для носителей языка. Где нужно измени и подправь. Чтобы было правильно для носителей "О значимости и истории этого изумрудной парюры для Датской королевской семьи мы подробно поговорим в этом видео."
In this video, we will discuss in detail the significance and history of this emerald parure for the Danish Royal Family.

Помоги структурировать следующий текст. Как и в какой последовательности лучше поведать историю датской изумрудной парюры, чтобы было и интересно и незапутанно. "Hello, everyone! Welcome back to my channel.
Recently, King Frederik and Queen Mary of Denmark released their inaugural official portraits as monarchs following Queen Margrethe II’s abdication in favor of her eldest son. On January 14, Frederik was proclaimed the new king; now, after three months in power, the royal couple have unveiled their inauguration portraits.
In these portraits, Mary officially makes her debut in one of the four parures from the Danish crown jewel collection. She wears the tiara, earrings, and necklace from the Danish Emerald Parure, as well as a modern emerald and diamond ring and her upgraded engagement and wedding rings.
In this video, we will discuss in detail the significance and history of this emerald parure for the Danish Royal Family.
Before we begin, please support my channel by clicking the like and subscribe buttons. Thank you!
The Emerald Parure is among most important Jewels at the use of the Danish Queen. Made in 1840 by C.M. Weisshaupt, the Parure features a Tiara composed of 67 emeralds and 2,650 diamonds, of which the largest 26 emeralds date from 1723, alongside a a pair of necklace, earrings, and a large brooch.
There is a great deal of history associated with this parure, which isn’t surprising given that it is part of the Crown Jewels.
More than a third of the green gemstones in the set can be traced back to an emerald necklace and a cross that Queen Sophie Magdalene received in 1723 from her husband, then Crown Prince Christian VI, to mark the birth of their first child, Frederik V. Sophie Magdalene will have cherished the emerald necklace and the cross, and as a gift at the birth of a future king. It seems only natural that the emeralds were included in the crown jewels.
It seems only natural that the emeralds were included in the crown jewels. However, not originally intended as crown jewels.
Sophie Magdalene did not leave the brightly coloured stones to the crown when she drew up her last will and testament in 1746. In fact, this document makes no mention of the stones at all. The decision to include the emeralds in the crown jewels – perhaps the best-known of them all – was made after Sophie Magdalene’s death by Queen Juliane Marie. With this step, she made sure that the emeralds would remain together as a set.
In 1849 the Absolute Monarchy was replaced by a Constitutional Monarchy. This made it necessary to determine what belonged to the state, and what belonged to the crown. The jewels weren’t as easy to divide as other items like property. Many jewels were created using both tax payer money and private royal money, so there were some compromises to be made. That’s how the emerald parure became part of the crown jewels.
Queen Alexandrine had the jewels in 1912 but in 1914 they were given to Rosenborg because she did not want to have them with her at the Amalienborg. She found she had her own private jewels and that was enough for her. Then 8 years after the museum made them part of the exhibition, but so that the queen could get them whenever she wanted. Well, appearently she never did. But Queen Ingrid coming from the splendid Swedish jewelry collections immidiately used her right to wear the crown jewels.
As stated earlier, The more modern emerald parure was created in 1840. These emeralds were put together with diamonds that already were owned by the Royal Family when the parure was commissioned in 1840.
In 1839, King Christian VIII, a great-grandson of Christian VI and Sophie Magdalene, came to the throne of Denmark. His queen consort was his second wife, Caroline Amalie of Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Augustenburg.
According to one version, the emerald parure was a gift from Queen Caroline Amalie on her 25th wedding anniversary.
According to another version, around the time of the coronation in 1840, Queen Caroline Amalie worked with the jeweler C.M. Weishaupt to renovate many of the eighteenth-century jewels in the royal vaults. Weisshaupt renovated the emeralds for the coronation festivities, adding additional gemstones that belonged to the King’s sister, Princess Charlotte of Denmark.
The fact is that the coronation was held in June 1840, a few weeks after the couple’s wedding anniversary.
Queen Caroline Amalie was not depicted in the Parure but left it to the Danish Crown Jewels upon her death in 1881, which means that it cannot be taken outside of Denmark or be worn by anyone other than the Queen Regnant or Queen Consort.
After Queen Caroline Amalie’s death in 1881, the Emerald Parure finally came into the possession of Queen Louise, who lack of suitable jewels had prevented her from attending the Wedding of her daughter, Princess Dagmar, to the future Tsar Alexander III of Russia. Queen Louise primarily wore a Diamond Tiara, and was only depicted in the Tiara of the Emerald Parure once, for a painting now at Vallø Slot.
Neither, Queen Lovisa (who had a large personal jewellery collection) nor Queen Alexandrine were pictured in the Crown Emerald Parure, but after the outbreak of the First World War in 1914, Queen Alexandrine sent the Danish Crown Jewels for safekeeping to Rosenborg Castle, where remain on permanent public display but remain at the disposal of the Queen.
It wasn’t until after the Accession of King Frederik IX of Denmark in 1947 that the Emerald Parure began to be publicly worn by the glamorous Queen Ingrid, who wore it for numerous occasions, including for the British State Visit to Denmark in 1957 and the Norwegian State Visit to Denmark in 1958.
Queen Margrethe began to wear the Emerald Parure soon after her Accession in 1972, including for the documentary ‘Margrethe, Queen of Denmark‘, when she was interviewed wearing the Parure. 
Her Majesty Queen Margrethe often wore the emerald set with green dresses that matched the colour of the stones. 
Queen Margrethe has said:
“I always feel a great responsibility since they are not personal property like other jewels, and they are exposed and shown at Rosenberg so the public will see them, so we don’t take them out for very long and not often either, but people also find it interesting when they are missing because they are actually being used and are not museum pieces”.
In 2023, Queen Margrethe wore the Emerald Parure for the final time at a State Banquet for King Felipe VI and Queen Letizia of Spain on their State Visit to Denmark.
On a delicate gold frame, the brilliants are set in closed-back silver mounts, while the large emeralds are set in open mounts to allow the light to strike the stones from both sides. The effect of the open mounts is particularly striking for the drop-shaped emeralds in the diadem and the large earring pendants. As the stones capture and reflect the light, it brings out the full splendour of the dark green emeralds. Their deep, uniform colour adds to the beauty of this set, which is almost always worn as a whole. The brooch can be disassembled, and the individual parts worn separately.
The Danish crown jewels belong to the state and are used exclusively by the Queen, but they don’t travel outside of Denmark as a general rule, meaning that Mary will only wear them for gala occasions on Danish soil.
Thank you for exploring these historical treasures with me. If you found this video enlightening, please support by liking, subscribing, and sharing. Don’t forget to hit the notification bell to stay updated on our latest content. Your engagement is greatly appreciated.

"

For a structured and engaging narrative on the Danish Emerald Parure's story, you might consider organizing the content as follows:

1. **Introduction**
   - Welcome viewers to the channel, mention the release of King Frederik and Queen Mary's official portraits, and introduce the topic of the Danish Emerald Parure's significance and history.

2. **Historical Background of the Danish Emerald Parure**
   - Briefly introduce the origins of the Emerald Parure, highlighting its creation in 1840 and its royal significance.
   - Mention the emeralds' history, dating back to 1723 from Queen Sophie Magdalene, to contextualize their royal and sentimental value.

3. **The Crown Jewels and the Parure's Role**
   - Explain the transformation of personal jewels to crown jewels and the legal and historical significance of this transition during the shift to a Constitutional Monarchy in 1849.
   - Share the story of Queen Juliane Marie's decision to include these emeralds in the crown jewels.

4. **Detailed Look at the Parure**
   - Describe the components of the parure (tiara, earrings, necklace, brooch) and their unique features, emphasizing the craftsmanship and beauty of these items.

5. **Owners Through History**
   - Starting with Queen Caroline Amalie and the speculation around the parure's creation.
   - Detail the parure's passage through the Danish royal family, focusing on notable wearers like Queen Louise, Queen Alexandrine, Queen Ingrid, and Queen Margrethe, and their personal stories related to the parure.

6. **Modern Significance**
   - Highlight Queen Margrethe's use and views on the crown jewels, leading to the recent appearance of Queen Mary in the emerald parure.
   - Discuss the rules regarding the wearing of these jewels and their role in Danish royal traditions today.

7. **Conclusion**
   - Sum up the cultural and historical importance of the Emerald Parure to the Danish Royal Family and Denmark.
   - Close by encouraging viewers to like, subscribe, and share for more content.

This structure flows from a broad introduction through historical context, detailed examination of the jewelry, personal stories of its wearers, to its continued relevance and concludes by engaging the viewer for further interaction. Each section builds on the last, providing a comprehensive yet clear and engaging narrative that keeps the viewer informed and entertained.

Sure, I'll enhance the introduction to make it more engaging and compelling for the viewers:

---

"Welcome, history aficionados and jewel lovers alike, to a tale wrapped in luxury, power, and the gleam of emeralds that have beheld centuries. Imagine, for a moment, treasures so captivating that they've adorned the necks, ears, and heads of Danish royalty through the winds of change and time.

Recently, a new chapter unfolded as King Frederik and Queen Mary of Denmark graced the world with their inaugural official portraits - a significant milestone since the venerable Queen Margrethe II chose to pass the torch of monarchy to her eldest son. Captured within these historic frames is Queen Mary, resplendent in the Danish Emerald Parure—the same jewels that whisper the secrets of regal legacy and tradition.

Why do these emerald gems captivate us so? What tales do they hold within their brilliant depths? Today, we embark on a journey not just through the annuls of Danish royalty but into the heart of a legacy crystalized in emerald green. From their mysterious origins, through the hands of those who wore them with pride and poise, to their significance within the Danish Crown Jewels, we uncover the saga of the Danish Emerald Parure.

But before we set sail on this voyage through time, lend your support to keep history alive on this channel—hit the like button, subscribe, and let the bell ring to not miss out on any enchanting stories from the past. Now, let the curtains rise on our tale of intrigue, elegance, and the eternal allure of the Danish Emerald Parure. Thank you for joining me!"

---

This reimagined introduction aims to draw viewers in with storytelling and the promise of uncovering historical secrets, providing an intriguing setup that encourages them to watch until the end.

In these portraits, Mary officially makes her debut in one of the four parures from the Danish crown jewel collection. She wears the tiara, earrings, and necklace from the Danish Emerald Parure, as well as a modern emerald and diamond ring and her upgraded engagement and wedding rings."

In a recent royal unveiling, King Frederik and Queen Mary of Denmark graced the public with their first official portraits as the reigning monarchs, marking a new era following Queen Margrethe II's decision to abdicate in favor of her eldest son. Ascending to the throne on January 14, Frederik alongside Mary, after three months at the helm, presented their inaugural portraits. 

In this historic depiction, Queen Mary is adorned with the Danish Emerald Parure, a dazzling ensemble from the royal Danish crown jewels, featuring a tiara, earrings, and a necklace. Complementing this regal attire, she also showcased a contemporary emerald and diamond ring along with her enhanced engagement and wedding bands, making a striking debut in royal finery.

"The Emerald Parure is among most important Jewels at the use of the Danish Queen. Made in 1840 by C.M. Weisshaupt
More than a third of the green gemstones (the largest 26 emeralds ) in the set can be traced back to an emerald necklace and a cross that Queen Sophie Magdalene received in 1723 from her husband, then Crown Prince Christian VI, to mark the birth of their first child, Frederik V. On a delicate gold frame, the brilliants are set in closed-back silver mounts, while the large emeralds are set in open mounts to allow the light to strike the stones from both sides. The Emerald Parure stands as one of the most significant jewels in the possession of the Danish Queen. Crafted in 1840 by C.M. Weisshaupt, this exquisite set is noteworthy for its historic and aesthetic value. A significant portion of the green gemstones, specifically the largest 26 emeralds, have origins dating back to an emerald necklace and cross gifted to Queen Sophie Magdalene in 1723 by her husband, then Crown Prince Christian VI, to commemorate the birth of their first child, Frederik V. This gift, imbued with sentimental value at the celebration of a future king’s birth, was profoundly treasured by Sophie Magdalene.

The remainder of the emeralds once belonged to the King's sister, Princess Charlotte, incorporating a familial legacy into the parure. In 1840, C.M. Weisshaupt expertly assembled the parure, enhancing its splendor with diamonds from the royal collection. The meticulous craftsmanship is evident in the delicate gold frame, where brilliants are set in closed-back silver mounts, and the sizable emeralds are placed in open mounts. This design choice allows light to pass through from both sides, highlighting the distinctive drop shape of the emeralds in the diadem and the large earring pendants, thus amplifying the emeralds' dark green splendor. The deep, uniform color of these emeralds enhances the set's overall beauty, typically worn together as a complete ensemble. Notably, the brooch offers versatility as it can be disassembled and the individual components worn separately, showcasing the thoughtful design and functionality of this historic parure.

"In 1839, King Christian VIII, a great-grandson of Christian VI and Sophie Magdalene, came to the throne of Denmark. His queen consort was his second wife, Caroline Amalie of Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Augustenburg.

 Around the time of the coronation in 1840, Queen Caroline Amalie worked with the jeweler C.M. Weishaupt to renovate many of the eighteenth-century jewels in the royal vaults. Weisshaupt renovated the emeralds for the coronation festivities, adding additional gemstones.

According to one version, the emerald parure was a gift from Queen Caroline Amalie on her 25th wedding anniversary. The fact is that the coronation was held in June 1840, a few weeks after the couple’s wedding anniversary."

In 1839, Denmark welcomed King Christian VIII to the throne, a direct descendant of Christian VI and Sophie Magdalene. His consort, Caroline Amalie of Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Augustenburg, became the queen consort following their marriage. 

Close to the 1840 coronation, Queen Caroline Amalie collaborated with the esteemed jeweler C.M. Weishaupt on a significant project to refurbish numerous 18th-century jewels housed within the royal treasury. This renovation effort included the emeralds set to be featured in the coronation celebrations, enhancing them with additional gemstones to elevate their grandeur.

There exists a narrative suggesting that the emerald parure was presented by Queen Caroline Amalie to her husband as a commemorative gift for their 25th wedding anniversary. Intriguingly, this period coincides closely with the coronation event that took place in June 1840, just a few weeks subsequent to the royal couple’s anniversary milestone.

"According to one version, the renovation of the set was intended to be an anniversary present. It’s possible: the coronation was held in June 1840, a few weeks after the couple’s 25th wedding anniversary."

One interpretation suggests that the refurbishment of the jewelry set was conceived as an anniversary gift. This theory holds weight, considering the coronation took place in June 1840, shortly following the royal couple's 25th wedding anniversary.
